content.text: ARM supports deployments at four distinct scopes: resource group, subscription, management group, and tenant. Each scope controls which resources can be managed, which deployment modes are available, and what RBAC permissions are required. The scope determines the ARM endpoint and the JSON schema used in the template.

The resource group scope is used for the vast majority of workloads. The broader scopes — subscription, management group, and tenant — are used for governance resources like policies, role assignments, and resource group creation.

Deploying to a resource group is the default for application infrastructure: virtual machines, storage accounts, databases, and networking components. The deployment inherits the resource group's location as its deployment metadata location; individual resources inside the template can still target any supported Azure region.

# Resource group deployment
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @prod.params.json \
  --name deploy-$(date +%Y%m%d)
Resource group deployments support both Incremental and Complete modes. The Complete mode applies only within the resource group boundary — it has no effect on resources in other resource groups.

Subscription-scoped deployments manage resources that span resource groups or set up governance structures. Common uses include creating resource groups themselves, assigning Azure Policy definitions and assignments, and applying role assignments across the subscription.

# Subscription-scoped deployment
az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file sub-level.bicep \
  --parameters environment=prod
The --location flag is required for subscription, management group, and tenant deployments. It sets where ARM stores the deployment metadata, not where the resources are created.

Management group deployments target a management group hierarchy node. They are used for enterprise-wide governance: applying Azure Policy definitions across multiple subscriptions, setting up role definitions that propagate down the hierarchy, and deploying shared governance templates.

# Management group deployment
az deployment mg create \
  --management-group-id myMG \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file mg-policy.bicep

Tenant-scoped deployments target the root of the Azure AD tenant and are used for the most global operations: creating management groups, assigning roles at the tenant root, and deploying tenant-level policy definitions. These deployments require Owner or User Access Administrator roles at the tenant root, which is the highest privilege level in Azure.

# Tenant-scoped deployment
az deployment tenant create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file tenant-mg.bicep
Tenant-scope deployments require the tenant root management group to be accessible. Run: az account management-group show --name <tenantId> to verify access before deploying.

A single Bicep template can deploy resources across multiple scopes using the scope property on modules and resource declarations. The targetScope in the template sets the default; individual resources or modules can override it.

In Terraform, the deployment scope is determined by the provider's subscription_id and auxiliary provider aliases rather than a deployment-time parameter. Cross-scope deployments use separate provider configurations.
Python SDK — Deployment Clients by Scope
The Azure SDK for Python provides separate deployment clients for each scope. All follow the same pattern: create a client for the scope, then call begin_create_or_update.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.resources.v2022_09_01.models import (
    Deployment, DeploymentProperties, DeploymentMode
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# Resource group scope
poller = client.deployments.begin_create_or_update(
    resource_group_name='myRG',
    deployment_name='my-deploy',
    parameters=Deployment(
        properties=DeploymentProperties(
            mode=DeploymentMode.INCREMENTAL,
            template=template_dict,
            parameters=params_dict,
        )
    )
)
result = poller.result()
print(f'Provisioning state: {result.properties.provisioning_state}')

# Subscription scope uses ResourceManagementClient.deployments_at_subscription_scope
sub_poller = client.deployments_at_subscription_scope.begin_create_or_update(
    deployment_name='sub-infra',
    parameters=Deployment(
        location='eastus',   # required for subscription scope
        properties=DeploymentProperties(
            mode=DeploymentMode.INCREMENTAL,
            template=sub_template,
        )
    )
)

The --what-if flag works at all four deployment scopes and previews the changes ARM would make without executing them. The result format is the same regardless of scope: resources listed as Create, Modify, Delete, or NoChange.

az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters environment=prod \
  --result-format FullResourcePayloads

az deployment sub what-if \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file sub-infra.bicep \
  --parameters @sub-params.json

az deployment mg what-if \
  --management-group-id myMG \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file mg-policies.bicep

az deployment tenant what-if \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file tenant-mg.bicep
Always run what-if before deploying governance templates at subscription, management group, or tenant scope — a policy assignment error at MG scope can block dozens of child subscriptions.
Granting Access to Deploy at Each Scope
Deploying at broader scopes requires explicit RBAC permissions that may not be granted by default. The table below shows the minimum role needed at each scope and how to assign it.
Elevating to Tenant Root Owner (Global Admin Only)

# A Global Administrator must first elevate access to see the tenant root scope
az rest --method post \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess?api-version=2016-07-01'

# Then assign Owner at root scope for the deployment service principal
az role assignment create \
  --scope '/' \
  --role 'Owner' \
  --assignee '<servicePrincipalObjectId>'

# After the deployment completes, revoke the elevated role:
az role assignment delete \
  --scope '/' \
  --role 'Owner' \
  --assignee '<servicePrincipalObjectId>'
Never leave permanent Owner at the tenant root scope for automated pipelines. Assign it, run the deployment, then revoke it immediately.
Each scope maintains its own deployment history. The CLI command to query history differs by scope and requires the scope identifier as an argument.

# List deployment history — resource group
az deployment group list --resource-group myRG -o table

# List deployment history — subscription
az deployment sub list -o table

# List deployment history — management group
az deployment mg list --management-group-id myMG -o table

# List deployment history — tenant
az deployment tenant list -o table

# Export a template from subscription history
az deployment sub export --name sub-infra-20240301

# Show failure details for a specific MG deployment
az deployment mg show \
  --management-group-id myMG \
  --name mg-policies-v2 \
  --query 'properties.error'
Nested Scopes in ARM Templates (ARM JSON)

In raw ARM JSON templates, cross-scope resource deployments use the Microsoft.Resources/deployments nested resource with a subscriptionId and/or resourceGroup property to redirect to a different scope. Bicep hides this complexity behind the scope keyword.

In this ARM JSON example, the nested deployment targets a specific subscription ID and resource group, even though the parent template may be running at subscription or management group scope. The nested deployment always runs in Incremental mode.
Deploy application VMs, storage, databases, and networking: use resource group scope.
Create resource groups programmatically or assign subscription-wide policies: use subscription scope.
Define or assign Azure Policy across multiple subscriptions in your org: use management group scope.
Create top-level management groups or assign roles at the tenant root: use tenant scope.
When in doubt, prefer the narrowest scope that satisfies your requirement — it minimizes blast radius and requires the least RBAC privilege.
Always test governance deployments (MG and tenant scope) in a non-production management group hierarchy first.
Each scope has unique error patterns that arise from insufficient permissions, missing location parameters, or attempting unsupported operations (like Complete mode at subscription scope).
Missing RBAC permission at the target scope
Assign the appropriate Contributor or Owner role at the scope
Add --location with a valid Azure region name
mode: Complete used at non-RG scope
Service principal lacks access to the nested scope
Assign role to SP at the nested subscription or RG
Management group ID not found or no access
Verify MG ID with: az account management-group list
Global Admin has not elevated to access tenant root

# Diagnose a failed deployment at subscription scope
az deployment sub show \
  --name failed-deploy \
  --query 'properties.{state:provisioningState,error:error}' \
  -o json

# Diagnose operation-level errors
az deployment operation sub list \
  --name failed-deploy \
  --query '[?properties.provisioningState==`Failed`].properties.statusMessage' \
  -o json
For nested cross-scope deployments, check the operation list of the inner nested deployment, not just the outer one — the outer deployment may show Succeeded while an inner module actually failed.

ARM's four deployment scopes — resource group, subscription, management group, and tenant — each map to a distinct CLI command, JSON schema, and set of capabilities. Resource group deployments are the most common and the only scope that supports Complete mode. The broader scopes target governance resources and require elevated RBAC permissions. In Bicep, the scope keyword on modules allows a single template to span multiple scopes cleanly.
